artifacts.content:
Elena,

Kids are finally asleep and I’m staring at Revit again, so I’m taking a break to write you instead. Harborview Promenade is turning into this weird collage of all our past projects and family stuff.

I pushed the team today to stay with pole‑top mounting only along the south façade, no wall‑mounted Olivio Grande heads. I told them flat out I’m not repeating Ruston Way March 2023, when that low wall‑mount RGBW layout plus the dynamic scenes set off Sofia’s severe migraine. I still hear Dr. Kaplan at Mary Bridge warning us after her October 2022 soccer concussion that rapid color‑changing in her peripheral vision could aggravate the post‑concussion symptoms. I don’t care if the architect wants “more sparkle” on that building; they can live without it.

At the same time, I’m reusing the mixed type layout from the October 2021 UW Pier 62 fundraiser job for the event pavilion—two pole‑top heads along the main walk, single wall‑mounts at the pavilion. That mix worked well there, and honestly, comping part of that fixture package out of the Harborline budget is still worth it in my head because it helped us cover Mamá’s $1,200 Lakewood rent while her Social Security review dragged on. I keep thinking about that when I start worrying about Harborline’s pipeline.

Tomorrow morning I’ve got another round of DMX layout coordination, then I’ll walk Sofia to school. Can you text me your Tacoma General shift for Friday? If you’re on a late one, I’ll see if my parents can still do their every‑other‑Friday babysitting so we don’t lose our 6th Ave tacos + Foss Waterway walk.

I’ll load the dishwasher; just leave your mug in the sink.

– D

metadata.subject: Harborview types, Sofia, and Friday night

artifacts.content:
Design / risk notes – pulled together before next Harborline Monday check‑in

1. Harborview Promenade – Type + risk memory dump
- South façade: keep it pole‑top only, no wall‑mounted Olivio Grande heads. Ruston Way waterfront March 2023 is still the cautionary tale: low wall‑mount RGBW + dynamic scenes = Sofia’s severe migraine episode, after Dr. Kaplan at Mary Bridge already warned about rapid color‑changing light in her peripheral vision post‑October 2022 soccer concussion.
- In tighter vehicular zones on future work, seriously consider wall‑mount only. June 2023 Marina Vista Apartments entry: 20‑ft pole type knocked over by drunk driver at 2 a.m. → six‑month insurance dispute with State Farm + threatened negligence claim that Laura Kim had to walk back. Don’t put tall poles where cars can realistically clip them.

2. Product modifications – extra pre‑wire lessons
- Standard going forward: request factory to pre‑wire universal mount heads with extra 10' of 18/5 SJOW cable. Grant Elementary playground (Tacoma School District) on March 14, 2024 is the reminder: field crew tied into wrong circuit → after‑hours safety incident → formal complaint to Harborline. Label key heads (e.g., “Playground East/West” or similar) near critical play areas.

3. Personal / scheduling overlay
- If Selux can ship heads labeled by pole (P‑1–P‑6), crew can keep installing even when I duck out for kid appointments (OT, neurology, etc.).
- Build these constraints into schedules instead of pretending evenings are free; they’re not, between Sofia’s sensitivities and Mateo’s anxiety at big events.

Stick this in Harborview project folder + personal “lessons learned.”

Hey Isa,

Catching up between DMX spreadsheets. I’ve been thinking about how many projects are tied up with our family lately.

For Harborview I’m doing a mixed type layout at the event pavilion—two pole‑top Olivio Grande heads per pole along the main promenade and single wall‑mounted heads at the pavilion. It’s basically the same distribution I used for that October 2021 UW alumni fundraiser at Pier 62. Remember when I quietly comped part of the fixture package out of the Harborline budget so we could free up cash to cover Mamá’s $1,200 rent at her senior apartment in Lakewood while her Social Security benefit review was delayed? I don’t regret it, but it still makes me hyper‑aware of every project budget.

On the hardware side I’m going back to that RAL 7016 Anthracite Grey trick—custom top caps and hardware, poles in standard BK. I first did that at Wright Park in June 2023 for Carlos’s memorial bench, when Linda Chavez from Parks & Rec told me on the June 14 walkthrough we only had $3,500 left from the wrongful‑death settlement fund you’re managing. Same idea now: keep poles standard to control cost, tweak the details to make it feel intentional.

Meanwhile, Dad’s arthritis is getting worse and he’s cut back his warehouse hours again. I’m still sending them a bit every month, but with Harborline losing that private development job in January I’m watching our own cash flow too. Elena’s shifts at Tacoma General are all over the place, and Sofia’s OT and follow‑ups at Mary Bridge aren’t exactly cheap.

Let’s plan a Sunday at Wright Park when the weather cooperates. Kids can hit the playground, and we can check on Carlos’s bench together.

Abrazos,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Pier 62 déjà vu and Carlos’s bench

artifacts.content:
Event: Harborview Terrace courtyard punch‑list with Dad

Date: 2025-03-15 (Saturday)
Time: 10:00 a.m. – 1:00 p.m.
Location: Harborview Terrace courtyard, 214 Harborview Terrace, Tacoma, WA 98402
Attendees: Daniel, Dad (Miguel), possibly Isabel (driver)

Notes:
- Meet Dad out front so he doesn’t have to walk the full loop; his arthritis has been flaring again and he’s still officially on light‑duty electrical work.
- I spec’d the Olivio Grande heads on this courtyard job with 10 ft of extra 18/5 SJOW cable per fixture and an internal junction point in the pole base specifically so he doesn’t need to climb a ladder or open the heads. After his minor stroke back in July 2022, I promised Mamá we’d keep him off ladders.
- Bring cordless drill, torque wrench, labeler, and the revised DMX address list. Double‑check base junctions are accessible from ground level and that the pre‑wire lengths are actually usable (no more “mystery coils” stuffed in poles).
- Take a few photos of Dad working from the ground for my own “designing for aging electricians” talk idea.
- Text Isabel around 9:15 to confirm she can drive him over and back; he still hates driving long distances since the stroke.
- Try to be home by 1:30 p.m. so Elena can get to Tacoma General for her shift and I can take Sofia to piano and Mateo to the park if it’s not pouring.

Reminder: bring Dad coffee (half‑caf) and his favorite cinnamon roll from Corina Bakery.

Mark,

Quick note while I’m updating the Selux submittals for Harborview Promenade.

I’m standardizing our DMX labeling approach based on a couple of projects you’d recognize. For Product Modifications Line 4 on the Olivio Grande heads, I’m asking Selux to factory‑install engraved stainless steel labels on each luminaire whip indicating DMX universe and address, attached within about 6" of the housing. Where the city will let me, I’m also having them add pole ID and circuit number so they line up with the as‑built set I submitted to City of Tacoma Planning on 11/03/2024 for the waterfront promenade next to my condo at 214 Harborview Terrace. We had to revise that layout after my neighbor’s slip‑and‑fall claim in the dim zone by Pier 3, and I don’t want any ambiguity the next time someone is troubleshooting scenes at 2 a.m.

I’m also asking Selux to match the DMX patch scheme we used on the pediatric oncology renovation at Tacoma General. If they engrave the addresses using the same convention as the patch sheet I sent you on 02/12/2025 for the chemo bays, we can basically reuse the night‑lighting scene logic without re‑addressing everything from scratch. That should make it easier for facilities staff who bounce between hospital and civic work.

Once I get revised shop drawings back with the engraving details called out, I’ll forward them your way in case you want to standardize this across other TG projects.

Hope the peds unit is running smoothly and you’re not getting too many 3 a.m. “why is it purple in here?” calls.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: DMX labeling – reusing oncology patch logic

artifacts.content:
Personal / work overlap – lighting + family mental health

1. Color + Sofia
- For Harborview and anything near our neighborhood waterfront, keep DMX scenes trauma‑informed. Ask factory to pre‑program Scene 1 = static warm white (3000K), Scene 2 = slow pastel rotation (~10‑minute cycle) with NO pure red, Scene 3 = event mode with saturated colors capped at 80% output.
- Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health was clear after Sofia’s March 2023 hit‑and‑run outside Stanley Elementary: strong red is a PTSD trigger. She reiterated it again in the 3/14/2024 session—avoid intense red lighting in places Sofia sees regularly.

2. Labels + trauma‑informed language
- On driver housings, relabel presets as “Calm Evening,” “Family Walk,” and “Event Mode” instead of Scene 1/2/3. That’s the terminology I used in the trauma‑informed lighting proposal to Tacoma City Council on May 9, 2024, when they even referenced Mateo’s anxiety episodes during evening events at Old Town waterfront park. The softer language actually helped them get it.

3. Electrical paranoia after condo mess
- Since the December 2022 panel overload at 214 Harborview (Dad’s heart‑monitoring setup per St. Joseph discharge, plus my temporary work lights) and the $4,800 emergency panel replacement, I’m more conservative on loads. For event scenes, consider asking manufacturers to limit max output to 70% and cap circuit loads around 9.5A where feasible. No more flirting with breaker limits.

Reminder: this isn’t just “work”; it’s literally the environment my kids grow up in.

metadata.title: Lighting + family mental health scratch note

artifacts.content:
Elena,

I know you’re slammed at Tacoma General tonight, but I wanted to brain‑dump a bit while the kids are watching their show.

I spent part of the afternoon arguing with the Selux rep about driver construction. After the January 2023 burst pipe at our Harborview Terrace condo shorted out that prototype driver I had in the living room mock‑up, I’m done taking chances. The PEMCO adjuster slapped “non‑UL field use” on the claim and we’re still in this slow‑motion dispute. So on Harborview I’m insisting the drivers are fully sealed and that they specify a conformal coating on the control boards anywhere we’re near spray or salt air. I do NOT want to give another insurer an excuse to deny anything.

On the controls side, I caught myself almost telling them to password‑protect the RDM interface with that same 8‑digit code I’ve been using since the 2019 IRS audit tied to my SSN ending 9173. It’s like my brain defaults to that number whenever I’m stressed. I actually wrote a note to myself today: stop reusing that code for anything—especially not DMX/RDM passwords or device locks on the Harborline office gear. Last thing I need is any weird overlap with the old audit documentation that’s still backed up from my old laptop.

Meanwhile, Sofia wants to see the pastel scenes again on my laptop, and Mateo’s already worried the “party colors” will make things too bright at the Old Town events. I told him we’re literally designing “Calm Evening” and “Family Walk” modes for kids like him.

Text me when you get a break so I know you ate something.

Love,
D

From: daniel.herrera@harborlinedesign.com
To: selux_orders@selux.com
Cc: project.team@harborlinedesign.com
Date: 2026-02-11 08:17
Subject: Harborview Promenade – Olivio Grande RGBW fixture notes (DMX + finishes)

Hi Selux team,

Following up on the Harborview Promenade RGBW package, I want to capture a few specific product modifications so they get reflected on the OLGR‑U / OLP‑5R submittals:

1) Type layout: Please confirm we can keep the combination of pole‑top and wall‑mounted heads, mirroring the mixed mounting approach we used on the February 2024 Harborline Design Studio pro‑bono installation at the Tacoma Recovery Outreach Center courtyard. That project used Olivio‑style RGBW heads to subtly highlight the entrance for the evening addiction support groups my father, Miguel Herrera, has been attending since his DUI case in Pierce County Superior Court last year.

2) Line 1 – Pre‑wire + ADA tag: Request factory to pre‑wire each universal mount head with an additional 10 ft of 18/5 SJOW cable and include a small engraved tag on each head reading “Herrera ADA Pathway – Low Glare.” We’re documenting that this layout was adjusted after the February 2023 slip‑and‑fall claim my mother, Patricia Herrera, filed at the Ruston Way waterfront in Tacoma, WA, when she tripped on a poorly lit path and I had to testify about inadequate illumination levels.

3) Line 2 – Color/finish: Please supply custom RAL 7016 Anthracite Grey top caps and hardware, but keep poles standard BK. I’m aligning this with similar security‑driven work I’ve done (Harborview Terrace HOA courtyard, St. Joseph Medical Center trauma‑recovery garden, and Pierce County Family Court supervised visitation courtyard) where we used RAL 7016 accents while maintaining standard black poles to stay within tight community and institutional budgets.

Let me know if any of the above requires a formal deviation letter.

Thanks,
Daniel Marcus Herrera
Senior Lighting Designer / PM
Harborline Design Studio, LLC

metadata.subject: Harborview Promenade – Olivio Grande RGBW fixture notes (DMX + finishes)

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.herrera@harborlinedesign.com
To: elena.herrera@gmail.com
Date: 2026-04-15 21:34
Subject: Courtyards, courts, and way too many RAL 7016 caps

Hey love,

I’m still at the laptop tweaking specs and needed to brain‑dump to someone who isn’t a city engineer.

I just wrapped notes for three different “heavy” courtyards that are all starting to blur together:

1) Harborview Terrace HOA courtyard here at 214 Harborview Terrace – the pro‑bono security/lighting upgrades I’ve been doing since October 2022 after our neighbor Mark Delaney was assaulted in the courtyard. At the October 18, 2022 HOA budget meeting in the community room, Karen Liu pushed hard to keep the poles in standard BK so we wouldn’t need another $25 per unit dues bump for the 36 apartments. I compromised by asking Selux for custom RAL 7016 Anthracite Grey top caps and hardware to match the benches and still make the place feel safer.

2) St. Joseph Medical Center trauma‑recovery garden, where Mom (Patricia) has been doing post‑stroke rehab since March 2023. In the March 27, 2023 facilities conference room meeting, Dr. Angela Kim liked my idea to echo the bench palette with RAL 7016 caps + hardware but insisted the poles stay BK so they wouldn’t have to re‑open the $42,000 capital budget for the garden upgrades.

3) Pierce County Family Court supervised visitation courtyard. At the April 12, 2024 coordination in Judge Morales’ chambers, guardian ad litem Rachel Nguyen asked for the same compromise: custom RAL 7016 caps/hardware, but standard BK poles because the $18,000 county grant from the domestic‑violence prevention program (the same pot helping your sister Isabel’s custody case support) can’t stretch to a fully custom pole package.

So yes, my life is now 50% parenting logistics, 50% arguing about pole finishes.

Heading home soon.

D.

metadata.subject: Courtyards, courts, and way too many RAL 7016 caps

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.herrera@harborlinedesign.com
To: dmx.team@harborlinedesign.com
Cc: city.facilities@cityoftacoma.org
Date: 2026-01-19 07:52
Subject: DMX labeling – reuse schemes from BH, Stadium HS, Sunrise Terrace

Team,

Before we lock the Harborview Promenade DMX notes, I want to standardize how we handle engraved stainless labels on the luminaire whips, pulling from a few recent projects:

1) Harborview Behavioral Health Courtyard (trauma‑therapy garden for Dr. Anika Patel, completed March 2023): For Product Modifications Line 4, we asked Selux to factory‑install engraved stainless labels on each whip with the DMX universe/address, using a clean font/layout that lets maintenance quickly cross‑reference the trauma‑therapy garden scenes. I’d like to reference that exact style on the new Olivio order.

2) Tacoma School District #10 – Stadium High School stadium retrofit (August 2022): After the security incident at the Fall 2022 homecoming game, we programmed emergency lockdown scenes and had the factory include custom engraved stainless labels on each luminaire whip showing DMX universe/address plus a short text code so the city electrician could match each fixture to the correct emergency lockdown scene. That clarity is the reason the district still calls us first when they tweak schedules.

3) Sunrise Terrace Senior Living assisted living courtyard (for my dad, Miguel Herrera, November 2023): When we reprogrammed the dusk‑to‑dawn schedule after his minor fall walking back from his 8:30 p.m. meds check, we engraved labels with DMX universe/address and the corresponding scene name so staff could immediately identify the brighter path scenes.

For Harborview, I propose we combine these approaches: engraved stainless labels with universe/address + short scene code, consistent with the BH courtyard and Stadium HS, and explicit “path bright”/“event pastel” naming like Sunrise Terrace.

Thoughts before I send this to Selux?

Daniel

metadata.subject: DMX labeling – reuse schemes from BH, Stadium HS, Sunrise Terrace

artifacts.content:

Laura,

As we finalize the Harborview Promenade RGBW package, I want to fold in a couple of lessons learned from the Tacoma School District work so we don’t repeat past mistakes.

Please have the factory pre-wire each Olivio Grande head with an extra 10 ft of 18/5 SJOW cable and clearly label the first two heads on Pole P-03 as “Playground East” and “Playground West” so our field crew doesn’t accidentally tie into the wrong circuit again like they did at the Tacoma School District’s Grant Elementary playground on March 14, 2024, which caused the after‑hours safety incident and the district’s formal complaint to my employer, Harborline Design Studio, LLC. I’d rather slightly over‑spec cable now than deal with another emergency call from the district.

For consistency, let’s extend the extra 10 ft of 18/5 SJOW cable per fixture to all Olivio Grande heads along the play areas and the small event plaza near the Murray Morgan Bridge. Same DMX/RDM driver setup we discussed: factory pre‑programmed scenes (static warm white, slow pastel rotation, and capped event mode).

I’m also updating our internal “Lessons Learned – K‑12” checklist to include mandatory head labeling whenever we’re near play courts, playgrounds, or school paths. Once I’ve cleaned that up, I’ll share it at the next Harborline Design Studio, LLC office lunch‑and‑learn.

Let me know if you see any coordination issues with the electrical engineer before I send this to Selux.

Thanks,
Daniel Marcus Herrera – Senior Lighting Designer / Project Manager
Harborline Design Studio, LLC – Tacoma, WA

metadata.subject: Harborview – Olivio Grande pre‑wiring + labeling (lessons from Grant Elementary)

artifacts.content:
Title: Grant Elementary – Lessons Learned (Tacoma School District)
Author: Daniel Marcus Herrera
Location: Harborline Design Studio, LLC office in Tacoma, WA
Date: 2024-03-20

1. Incident summary
- On March 14, 2024, at the Tacoma School District’s Grant Elementary playground, our field crew tied into the wrong circuit serving part of the play area lighting.
- Result: short after‑hours safety incident (portion of playground dark during an evening PTA event), followed by a formal complaint to my employer, Harborline Design Studio, LLC.
- No injuries, but not acceptable from a safety or reputation standpoint.

2. Root causes
- Inadequate labeling at pole heads; heads not clearly identified as “Playground East” vs. “Playground West.”
- Limited slack in factory wiring made it harder for the crew to re‑route heads once on site.

3. Required product modifications (going forward)
- Please have the factory pre-wire each Olivio Grande head with an extra 10 ft of 18/5 SJOW cable and clearly label the first two heads on Pole P-03 as “Playground East” and “Playground West” so our field crew doesn’t accidentally tie into the wrong circuit again.
- Standardize on 10 ft of 18/5 SJOW cable per fixture for any K‑12 playground or court lighting, even if it’s a VE item.

4. Process changes
- Add a “playground circuit check” step to pre‑construction meetings with the electrical subcontractor.
- Require a quick after‑hours walk‑through with school staff on the first night of operation.

– Daniel

metadata.title: Grant Elementary – Lessons Learned (Tacoma School District)
metadata.location: Harborline Design Studio, LLC office in Tacoma, WA
